summary:
• Geopolitical tensions with Iran, including U.S. strikes and threats to close the Strait of Hormuz, pose acute supply disruption risks to global oil markets with prices potentially climbing toward $150, while traders appear underestimating these risks.

• OPEC crude production has fallen to its lowest level since 2000, while Venezuela's oil industry reconstruction and limited new project sanctioning suggest sustained supply tightness that could support elevated pricing.

• Refining and logistics constraints, including Trans Mountain pipeline capacity limits and alternative supplier development (Japan's reserve taps, Canada's LNG challenges), indicate structural supply-route vulnerabilities limiting market flexibility to address disruptions.
